content: The perennial border is at its absolute best this week — I walked by on Tuesday and stood there for ten minutes. 'June' is extraordinary this year.
I have been meaning to add 'White Swan' echinacea to my border for two years. Your post finally convinced me. Ordering this week.
Linda, Tom at Glastonbury Garden Center still had 'White Swan' in stock as of last Saturday. Worth a call before ordering online.
The Siberian iris timing has been off for everyone this year — mine finished a full two weeks early too. Warm April, I think.

Reminder to everyone: I have confirmed the Community Center main hall for October 17. Eight-foot tables will be set up — plan for roughly six to eight plants per table length if you are packing tight.
I am planning to bring at least two flats of milkweed seedlings this year. Labeling them now so I do not forget the species name.
Peggy, I will ask Michael Egan about a bucket station near the door. Good point — I have the same concern about my cinnamon fern divisions.
I love the image of standing at the kitchen door with decaf watching the light change on wet hosta leaves. That is exactly the kind of morning this garden gives you.
Diatomaceous earth is my go-to for slugs too. I reapply after every heavy rain. Worth it to keep those crowns pristine.
I have had 'June' for twelve years and it still catches every visitor's eye. Your explanation of the sport origin is the clearest I have read.
Margaret, I am already drafting it — should be ready for your review before the June 20 newsletter deadline.
I went to the 2025 swap as a first-time attendee and came home with five plants. The relaxed atmosphere was exactly what the post describes. Already planning my bring-list for this October.
The tip about waiting until the eyes are just visible but leaves are not yet unfurled is something I have been getting wrong for years. Thank you for the correction.
Agreed. I have Christmas fern under the silver maple in the side yard and it is the most carefree planting I own.
Thank you all so much for coming. The Karl Foerster is at its best right now — please come back in August when the Miscanthus 'Morning Light' is in full plume.
Elena, I cut it back in early April — the dried blades are quite beautiful through winter so I leave them. It is reliably hardy here, Zone 6b has not given it trouble in eight years.
We just got a restock of 'Sombrero Salsa' — three sizes, 4-inch through gallon. Members with your card get 10% off this week only.
The point about not mounding mulch against stems is one I have to remind myself every single spring. This is a good refresher.
Linking to this post from the club website's Resources page for new members. Exactly the kind of practical advice we want to archive.
The lean-soil recommendation for echinacea is spot on. We tell customers the same thing and they are always surprised. Rich soil gives you floppy stems that need staking.
